step2_rag_chunks: Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step2_rag_chunks: Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step2_rag_chunks: When a patient has tried to quit multiple times and keeps relapsing, or reports strong physical cravings that are hard to manage, nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum or patches is worth discussing as an additional support tool alongside behavioral strategies.
Talking to someone about your cravings can help reduce the intensity of the urge.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
